title: Lancashire hotpot
subtitle: Lamb and kidney under layered potatoes.
excerpt: Diced lamb neck and lamb's kidneys layered with onions and carrots, topped with overlapping slices of potato brushed with butter, and slow-baked until the meat is tender and the lid of potatoes is gold-brown and crisp at the edges. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136), 'Lancashire Hot Pot'. The dish belongs to nineteenth-century Lancashire mill towns, designed to cook unattended in the residual heat of a fire while the household worked.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool, portion, freeze up to three months. The potato lid softens on reheating; expect a slightly different texture but the same flavour.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the recipe across two pots; one pot holds four portions comfortably with the potato lid as the cap.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble the layers raw, cover, and refrigerate up to a day. Bring back to room temperature for 30 minutes before the oven.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan 140°C; finished with the lid off at 200°C for the last 20 minutes

recipeTools.notes: Heavy lidded casserole; the slow oven and the tight lid are the point.
For even potato slices; a sharp knife works.

content.text: A heavy lidded pot, layers of seasoned lamb and kidney over sweated onions and carrots, a roof of overlapping potato slices brushed with butter, the whole baked slowly for two hours and finished with the lid off at high heat to crisp the top. Two and a half hours in the oven, almost all of it unattended. Plain food cooked patiently.

Neck of lamb is the cut: cheap, full of flavour, packed with connective tissue that melts to silk over a low bake. The kidneys give the dish its deep iron note; leave them out and the hotpot is still good, but it's not quite the Beeton original.

items.prepNote: cored, cut into 2 cm pieces (or 4 lamb's kidneys)
Maris Piper, peeled and sliced 3 mm

content.text: Toss {{lamb-neck}} of lamb neck cubes and the {{ox-kidney}} of kidney pieces in {{plain-flour}} of seasoned flour. Heat the oven to 160°C (140°C fan).

Soften {{onion}} of sliced onions and {{carrot}} of sliced carrots in a knob of butter in the casserole over a low hob for 10 minutes until translucent and just colouring. Lift two-thirds onto a plate; leave a layer in the bottom of the pot.

Scatter half the meat over the onion layer. Add half the remaining vegetables, half the {{thyme}} of thyme, and one {{bay-leaves}} of bay. Season. Repeat with the rest of the meat, vegetables, herbs, and the second bay leaf. Pour over the {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire and {{stock-beef}} of hot stock; it should come halfway up the meat layer.
Arrange {{potato}} of thinly sliced potatoes in overlapping rounds across the top, like roof tiles. Brush generously with {{unsalted-butter}} of melted butter. Season with salt and pepper.
Lid on. Bake for 1 hour 45 minutes. The meat softens; the stock reduces a little; the bottom layer of potatoes thickens the gravy as they cook.

Lift the lid. Raise the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Brush the potato top with the rest of the butter. Bake for a further 20 minutes until the potatoes are deep gold and crisp at the edges. Rest 15 minutes before serving.

items.cause: Lid left on too long, or butter wash skipped.
items.fix: Brush butter on twice — once at the start, once when the lid comes off. Always finish uncovered at 200°C.

items.cause: Not enough liquid, or oven too hot.
items.fix: Stock should reach halfway up the meat layer. 160°C is the ceiling for the slow phase.

items.cause: Skipped the flour dust on the meat.
items.fix: Toss the lamb in seasoned flour before layering. The flour thickens the gravy as it bakes.
Cut kidneys open, snip out the white core. Soak briefly in milk if you find the flavour strong.

items.symptom: Potatoes raw under the gold top layer
items.cause: Sliced too thick, or stock too low.
items.fix: Slice no thicker than 3 mm; a mandoline helps. Make sure liquid reaches halfway up the meat.

content.text: Lancashire hotpot is mill-town food. Cottages with cast-iron ovens, families at work all day, supper waiting on a low fire. The potato lid was once a way to seal in heat without a casserole; the modern Dutch oven does the same. Served as the centrepiece of a wet-Sunday lunch with pickled red cabbage and a wedge of crusty bread, it is the kind of cooking that asks little and gives much.
